perturbation.original_text: RECIPIENT agrees to keep confidential and refrain from disclosing any an d all of the INFORMATION, and to take all necessary and reasonable steps to prevent unauthorized disclosure or use of any and all of the INFORMATION. C. Notwithstanding paragraph B, RECIPIENT shall not be liable for disclosure or use of INFORMATION only if, and only to the extent that, said INFORMATION was in the public domain at the time it was disclosed by OWNER, or was known to and recorded in writing by RECIPIENT prior to the time of disclosure by OWNE R, or is received from a third party or passes into the public domain without breach of this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: RECIPIENT agrees to keep confidential and refrain from disclosing any an d all of the INFORMATION, and to take commercially reasonable steps to prevent unauthorized disclosure or use of any and all of the INFORMATION. C. Notwithstanding paragraph B, RECIPIENT shall not be liable for disclosure or use of INFORMATION if said INFORMATION was in the public domain at the time it was disclosed by OWNER, or was known to RECIPIENT prior to the time of disclosure by OWNE R, or is received from a third party or passes into the public domain without breach of this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: By changing 'all necessary and reasonable steps' to 'commercially reasonable steps', the required standard of care is lowered. Also, by removing 'and only to the extent that' and 'and recorded in writing by' in section C, the exception for prior knowledge becomes less stringent, contradicting the overarching goal of confidentiality. This creates an internal contradiction by weakening the recipient's obligation to protect the information. Section II (A)(3) requires 'all other reasonable means' which conflicts with 'commercially reasonable steps'.
perturbation.contradicted_text: II. RESTRICTIONS A. Except for the express written consent of OWNER, RECIPIENT agrees: 1. Not to use or disclose to another person or entity any confidential information of OWNER; 2. Not to make, or cause to be made, any copies , facsimiles or other reproductions including data files of any documents containing confidential information of OWNER; and 3. To use all other reasonable means to mainta in the secrecy and confidentiality of the confidential information of OWNER. B. RECIPIENT further agrees, at the request of OWNER: 1. To immediately return to OWNER all of the items in the possession of RECIPIENT which relate to or which disclose in whole or in part any confidential information of OWNER; and 2. To refrain from using or disclosing to any other person or entity any confidential information of OWNER.
perturbation.justification: The change from 'all necessary and reasonable steps' to 'commercially reasonable steps' weakens the standard of care required of the RECIPIENT. Further, removing 'and only to the extent that' and 'and recorded in writing by' makes the exception for prior knowledge less strict. This directly conflicts with section II(A)(3) which requires 'all other reasonable means', creating a strong contradiction because the obligation to maintain secrecy is now lessened, making the original requirement in Section II(A)(3) unenforceable to the same degree.

perturbation.original_text: All products and results of RECIPIENT’S services rendered hereunder (the "Work") are works made for hire. RECIPIENT acknowledge s and agree that the Work (and all rights therein, including, without limitation, copyrights) belongs to and shall be the sole and exclusive property of OWNER.
perturbation.changed_text: All products and results of RECIPIENT’S services rendered hereunder (the "Work") may be considered works made for hire. RECIPIENT acknowledges and agrees that the Work (and all rights therein, including, without limitation, copyrights) belongs to OWNER.
perturbation.explanation: By changing 'are works made for hire' to 'may be considered works made for hire' and removing 'shall be the sole and exclusive property of OWNER' the ownership of 'the work' is made ambiguous. This undermines the subsequent assignments of rights and creates an internal contradiction, especially with respect to the copyright assignment clauses in the section.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 2. Not withstanding the foregoing, RECIPIENT also hereby assigns and transfers to OWNER, its successors and assigns, the entire right, title, and interest in and to all copyrights in the Work; all registrations and copyright applications relating thereto and all renewals and extensions thereof; all works ba sed upon, derived from, or incorporating the Work; all income, royalties, damages, claims an d payments now or hereafter due or payable with respect thereto; all causes of action, either in law or in equity for past, present, or future infringement based on the copyrights; and all rights corresponding to the foregoing throughout the world.
perturbation.justification: The original text asserts that the 'Work' is 'made for hire' and belongs exclusively to the OWNER. The changed text weakens this statement by saying the work 'may be considered' work for hire and removes the section that says it shall be the property of the OWNER, introducing ambiguity about ownership. This directly contradicts the contradicted text (Section 2), which assigns all copyrights in the Work to the OWNER. If the Work's ownership is uncertain, the copyright assignment becomes problematic and contradictory.

perturbation.original_text: RECIPIENT hereby assigns and/or transfers to OWNER, its successors or assigns, the entire right, title, and interest in and to said Inventions, and any patent and patent applications deriving there from for any such invention in the United States and 3throughout the world, including the right to file foreign applications directly in the name of OWNER and to claim for any such foreign applications any priority rights to which such applications are entitled under international conventions, treaties, or otherwise; and to cooperate with OWNER as may be necessary or desirable for obtaining, sustaining, reissuing, or enforcing said patent or patent applications in the United States and throughout the world for said Inventions, and for perfecting, recording, or maintaining any such title in OWNER.
perturbation.changed_text: RECIPIENT hereby transfers to OWNER, its successors or assigns, the interest in said Inventions conceived solely and exclusively in the United States, and any patent applications deriving therefrom for any such invention in the United States, including the right to file applications directly in the name of OWNER. RECIPIENT will cooperate with OWNER to enforce the patents.
perturbation.explanation: By removing 'assigns and/or' and restricting it only to transfers, it raises a question of whether the recipient is assigning the patent rights or just transferring them. Removing the rights to patent outside the United States, limiting cooperation and restricting the inventions that applies to. This change contradicts the original intent of the agreement to secure intellectual property rights globally, as the omitted clause gave OWNER greater control, creating ambiguity that can result in dispute.
perturbation.contradicted_text: IV. COVENANT NOT TO SUE RECIPIENT shall not institute any action or suit at law or in equity against OWNER, nor institute, prosecute or in any way aid in the institution or prosecution of any claim, demand, action, or cause of action arising out of the INFORMATION or any INTELLECTUAL PROPERTY thereof, including but not limited to, claim, demand, action, or cause of action for in validating any INTELLECTUAL PROPERTY of OWNER.
perturbation.justification: The changed text significantly narrows the scope of intellectual property assignment to inventions conceived solely and exclusively in the United States, removing the global aspect present in the original text. This directly contradicts section IV, which includes a covenant not to sue regarding 'any INTELLECTUAL PROPERTY' of OWNER, regardless of geographic origin. By limiting the assignment to US-based inventions, the changed text creates ambiguity regarding the status of intellectual property rights outside the US, potentially undermining the OWNER's ability to enforce those rights and thus contradicting the broad protection intended by the covenant not to sue. This aligns with the definition of 'Omissions – In Text Contradiction' as essential language has been removed or altered, creating a contradiction with obligations stated in other sections.

perturbation.original_text: RECIPIENT agrees that should RECIPIENT breach a ny of the promises contained in this Agreement that OWNER would suffer irreparable harm and OWNER w ould be without adequate remedy at law and that OWNER may obtain injunctive relief, including specific performance of the Agreement, as well as monetary award for damages suffered by OWNER for RECIPIENT’S breach of this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: RECIPIENT agrees that should RECIPIENT breach any of the promises contained in this Agreement that OWNER may suffer harm and that OWNER may obtain monetary compensation for RECIPIENT’S breach of this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: By removing 'irreparable harm and OWNER would be without adequate remedy at law and that OWNER may obtain injunctive relief, including specific performance of the Agreement, as well as', the agreement no longer provides the owner with recourse to obtain injunctive relief or specific performance, limiting their remedies to monetary compensation. This conflicts with the general objective of protecting intellectual property rights, which injunctive relief would better facilitate. By deleting that the harm may be 'irreparable', it undermines the premise that the agreement is vital to protecting vital business assets. This creates an internal contradiction with the NDA's core intent.
perturbation.contradicted_text: I. CONFIDENTIAL INFORMATION A. OWNER agrees to disclose INFORMATION to RE CIPIENT to facilitate possible future business dealings between the parties. B. RECIPIENT agrees to receive such INFORMAT ION and to refrain from copying, disclosing, using, selling, or offering for sale any and all of said INFORMATION, other than at the request of OWNER, with the exceptions as provided in paragraph C herein. RECIPIENT agrees to keep confidential and refrain from disclosing any an d all of the INFORMATION, and to take all necessary and reasonable steps to prevent unauthorized disclosure or use of any and all of the INFORMATION.
perturbation.justification: The original text explicitly allowed the OWNER to seek injunctive relief and specific performance, indicating a strong protective stance for the disclosed confidential information. The changed text removes this right, limiting the OWNER's recourse to monetary compensation. This contradicts the contradicted text (I. CONFIDENTIAL INFORMATION) which emphasizes the importance of keeping the information confidential and preventing unauthorized disclosure. Monetary compensation may not adequately address a breach of confidentiality, especially if the information is crucial to future business dealings. The removal of injunctive relief creates a direct conflict, weakening the protection of confidential information outlined in the contradicted text. The Change means monetary compensation is the only thing the owner can seek, and the contradiction is that monetary compensation is not enough.

perturbation.original_text: Notwithstanding this paragraph, RECIPIENT shall honor all prior obligations conc erning confidentiality of OWNER’S confidential INFORMATION.
perturbation.changed_text: RECIPIENT shall endeavor to honor all prior obligations concerning confidentiality of OWNER’S confidential INFORMATION.
perturbation.explanation: By changing 'shall honor' to 'shall endeavor to honor' weakens the obligation to prior confidentiality agreements. This undermines the certainty expected of a merger clause, creating an internal contradiction because the original clause was designed to remove ambiguity and fully integrate past obligations into the current agreement. Since endeavor is not an obligation, and it is a suggestion, it creates an in-text contradiction with the term obligation on other locations in the text, where it is demanding the reipient to follow the terms of the contract, but it is not demanding for the previous agreements, or there is no certainty that the recipient will honor the prior agreement.
perturbation.contradicted_text: I. CONFIDENTIAL INFORMATION A. OWNER agrees to disclose INFORMATION to RE CIPIENT to facilitate possible future business dealings between the parties. B. RECIPIENT agrees to receive such INFORMAT ION and to refrain from copying, disclosing, using, selling, or offering for sale any and all of said INFORMATION, other than at the request of OWNER, with the exceptions as provided in paragraph C herein. RECIPIENT agrees to keep confidential and refrain from disclosing any an d all of the INFORMATION, and to take all necessary and reasonable steps to prevent unauthorized disclosure or use of any and all of the INFORMATION.
perturbation.justification: The original text mandates that the recipient 'shall honor' prior confidentiality obligations, whereas the changed text weakens this to 'shall endeavor to honor'. This introduces a contradiction with the contradicted text, which demands that the recipient 'agrees to keep confidential' and 'take all necessary and reasonable steps' to prevent unauthorized disclosure. The change from 'shall honor' to 'shall endeavor to honor' creates ambiguity and weakens the obligation, making it uncertain whether the recipient will fully comply with the prior confidentiality agreements, directly conflicting with the stricter requirements outlined in the contradicted text. Since 'endeavor' is not an obligation, the recipient may or may not honor the prior agreement.
